metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'single-culprit' threshold in `blame.py`'s spec: state explicitly whether blame must resolve to 100% of lines from one commit (or another concrete rule) before the link is emitted, so the executor has no design decision to make.
evidence: 'Resolves the dominant culprit commit' — no definition of dominant
'multi-commit spread' listed as an ambiguity case — no threshold stated (2 commits? any spread at all?)
ABSENCE: no percentage, line-count, or commit-count threshold is given anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan references a 'dominant culprit commit' and 'multi-commit spread' ambiguity threshold but never defines what makes a blame result 'single-culprit': is it 100% of blamed lines pointing to one commit, a majority, or something else? An executor must invent this threshold, which is a real design decision.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` description; Approach section: 'links only on a single dominant culprit'
scenarios: Executor implements '≥50% of lines' while the author meant '100% of lines', leading to incorrect auto-links on partially-refactored files
A reviewer/tester cannot write a deterministic fixture without knowing the exact threshold
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rule such as: 'single-culprit = ALL blamed lines in `file_impact` resolve to the same commit SHA; any divergence (≥2 distinct commits) is treated as multi-commit spread and returns None.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion for the `docs/user-guide.md` advisory, e.g. 'the user guide contains a `## Blame-Hunt Advisory` section explaining when to use `--caused-by` and when auto-derivation fires' (verifiable via `grep -A5 'Blame-Hunt Advisory' docs/user-guide.md`).
evidence: 'docs/user-guide.md — bug-template blame-hunt advisory' listed in Files
ABSENCE: none of the three ACs mention verifying the docs advisory exists or contains required guidance
findings.finding: The `docs/user-guide.md` bug-template blame-hunt advisory is listed as a required deliverable in the Files section but no Acceptance Criterion covers it. The union of ACs (test pass, link present/absent, --caused-by) does not verify this file was written or is correct.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/user-guide.md`; Acceptance Criteria section (all three ACs are test-only)
scenarios: An executor ships the code changes but omits the docs update; all three ACs pass and the ticket is closed without the advisory ever being written
A reviewer has no criterion against which to evaluate the doc content
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `grep -q 'caused-by' docs/user-guide.md && grep -q 'blame' docs/user-guide.md` or a stronger structural check against a required heading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number citation in the transition_close.py description from `_links.py:242-247` to `_links.py:252-257` (the actual closed-source `raise ValueError` block).
evidence: Plan states: 'graph._links.add_dependency rejects links from a closed source (_links.py:242-247 raises ValueError)'
Actual closed-source guard: src/rebar/graph/_links.py:252-257 ('if source_status == "closed": raise ValueError')
Lines 242-247 contain the CyclicDependencyError self-reference raise, not the closed-source guard
findings.finding: The plan reference `_links.py:242-247` for the closed-source guard is off by ~10 lines: the actual `source_status == 'closed'` raise is at lines 252-257. Minor, but if an implementer uses line numbers to locate the guard they will land in the cycle-check block instead.
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — CRITICAL ordering/guard note citing '_links.py:242-247'
scenarios: Implementer navigates to the wrong location when verifying the guard's semantics or adding a test assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `_links.py:242-247` with `_links.py:252-257` in the CRITICAL ordering/guard note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `blame.py` discovers `<fixing-commit>`: e.g., 'the most recent commit in git log whose message resolves to the bug ticket via `extract_ticket_refs` + `resolve_ticket_id`', and clarify tie-breaking/multi-commit behavior (should resolve → None on multi-commit, consistent with the stated ambiguity-abstain policy).
evidence: Plan states blame runs over '<fixing-commit>~1' but gives no algorithm for discovering <fixing-commit>
transition_close._referencing_commit_exists returns bool, not a commit SHA — it cannot be reused as-is to obtain the fixing commit
If multiple commits reference the same bug ticket, there is no specified tie-breaking rule
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how `blame.py` identifies the *fixing commit* (`<fixing-commit>` in `<fixing-commit>~1`). The close path's existing `_referencing_commit_exists` scans the full git log for *any* commit referencing the ticket and returns True/False, not a single commit SHA. Without a specified mechanism to resolve the fixing commit, the plan's core blame logic — "the parent of the fixing commit" — has an undefined input.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`; plan phrase 'the parent of the fixing commit (`<fixing-commit>~1`)'
scenarios: Bug fixed in two commits (squash not enforced): blame.py has no defined input and cannot produce a result
Fixing commit is a merge commit: `~1` selects the first parent, potentially giving wrong blame
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `blame.py` spec: 'Identify the fixing commit by scanning `git log --format=%H%x00%B` for the most recent commit whose message yields the bug's ticket id via `extract_ticket_refs` + `resolve_ticket_id`. If zero or ≥2 such commits are found, return None (ambiguous fixing commit).' This is consistent with the plan's existing conservative/abstain-on-ambiguity stance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies `docs/user-guide.md` contains the blame-hunt advisory — e.g. assert the file exists and mentions `caused_by` / `--caused-by` — so the doc deliverable is covered.
evidence: Files section lists: 'docs/user-guide.md — bug-template blame-hunt advisory'
None of the three ACs reference docs/user-guide.md or the blame-hunt advisory content
findings.finding: The described deliverable `docs/user-guide.md` (bug-template blame-hunt advisory) has no covering acceptance criterion — there is no AC that verifies the doc was added, its content is correct, or that it references the auto-derivation behavior.
findings.location: Files section: `docs/user-guide.md`; Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
scenarios: Implementer ships code changes but omits or misspecifies the user-guide update with no AC gate to catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `docs/user-guide.md` contains a blame-hunt advisory section referencing `caused_by` auto-derivation and `--caused-by` override (verified by grep or doc-review).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_links.py` line citation from `242-247` to `252-257` (the actual closed-source `ValueError` guard in `add_dependency`) so the bypass rationale is verifiable at the cited location.
evidence: Plan: 'graph._links.add_dependency rejects links from a closed source (`_links.py:242-247` raises ValueError)'
_links.py lines 242-247 (actual): CyclicDependencyError raise for self-referential dep in check_cycle_at_level block
_links.py lines 252-257 (actual): `source_status = _get_ticket_status(source_id, tracker_dir)` / `if source_status == 'closed': raise ValueError(...)`
findings.finding: The plan cites `_links.py:242-247` as the location where `add_dependency` raises `ValueError` for a closed-source ticket. Reading the file confirms the closed-source guard is actually at lines 252-257; lines 242-247 are the `check_cycle_at_level` `CyclicDependencyError` block for self-referential dependencies. The cited line range is incorrect.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — '`_links.py:242-247` raises ValueError'
scenarios: An implementer reading this plan who navigates to _links.py:242-247 to understand the guard being bypassed will find cycle-check logic, not the closed-source guard, and may misidentify what the direct-writer path is actually skipping.
If a future refactor moves lines, the wrong reference could cause confusion about whether the guard was re-introduced.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`_links.py:242-247` raises ValueError' to '`_links.py:252-257` raises ValueError' in the Files section description for `transition_close.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify validation for `--caused-by <id>`: existence check (ticket must resolve in the store), rejection of self-referential IDs, and a clear CLI error message on failure — before the close transition commits.
evidence: AC line 3: '`rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class regression --caused-by <id>` sets the link regardless of blame' — only happy path tested
Plan says same direct-writer path is used for `--caused-by`, which bypasses cycle guards — a self-referential or cycle-inducing ID would be silently accepted
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` parameter has no specified validation: the plan does not address what happens when the user supplies a non-existent, malformed, or self-referential ticket ID, leaving the error path unspecified for a user-facing input.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — AC line 3 / Files section
scenarios: User typos the ticket ID; close succeeds but the caused_by link points to a non-existent ticket
User passes the bug's own ID as `--caused-by`; self-referential link is written with no error

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git blame` output format (`--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain`) in `blame.py`'s design note so implementers use the only reliably machine-parseable format and the test fixture exercises the same path.
evidence: No 'git blame' subprocess calls exist anywhere in src/rebar (search returned zero hits)
No --porcelain or output-format specification is mentioned in the plan
git blame default output format is not reliably parseable by SHAs — only --porcelain guarantees stable commit hash extraction per line
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'git blame <fixing-commit>~1' over file_impact paths will yield parseable, per-line commit SHAs, but does not specify the git blame output format. The default git blame output is human-readable prose, not machine-parseable; only --porcelain or --line-porcelain reliably yields commit SHAs per blamed line. No prior blame subprocess exists in src/rebar to establish a format convention.
findings.location: Files section: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the pre-fix state'
scenarios: Implementation chooses default git blame output and writes an ad-hoc parser that breaks on filenames with spaces, binary hunks, or uncommitted changes
Implementation uses --porcelain but the plan provides no guidance, leaving the format choice undocumented and potentially mismatched with the test fixture
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Uses `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each output block's first line is the 40-char commit SHA, which is parsed directly without regex over human-readable output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git blame` output format in `blame.py`'s design (e.g., `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain`) and confirm the plan's commit-SHA extraction algorithm matches that format, so the implementation doesn't silently no-op on real repos.
evidence: Search for 'git blame' in src/rebar returns zero hits — this is a first-use of git blame in the codebase, so no format convention is established
Plan phrase 'Blames the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fixing-commit>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact' relies on extracting per-line commit SHAs from git blame output
Plan mentions 'rename/--follow uncertainty' as an abstain case but does not name --porcelain or any structured output flag
git blame default output format interleaves commit hashes with author/filename/line metadata in a non-fixed-column layout; --porcelain outputs one 40-char SHA per hunk header in a stable format
Evidence class: Partially-verified — git blame --porcelain existence is well-known, but no prior usage in this repo to confirm the project's convention
findings.finding: The plan introduces `git blame` as a new external CLI surface (zero existing `git blame` calls in src/rebar) but does not specify the output format flag. The default `git blame` output is human-readable prose; only `--porcelain` or `--line-porcelain` reliably yields stable, per-line commit SHA blocks machine-parseable enough to extract the culprit SHA without an ad-hoc regex. Without specifying the format, the implementer may choose the wrong format and produce a fragile parser that silently falls back to None on any moderately complex blame output (spaces in filenames, boundary commits, uncommitted-change markers), defeating the feature.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` — 'single-culprit resolver. Blames the **pre-fix** state … over the bug's recorded `file_impact`'
scenarios: Implementer uses default git blame output and writes a regex over the human-readable prefix; breaks on filenames containing spaces or parentheses (common in Python repos)
Test fixture uses --porcelain but production code uses a different format — tests pass but production silently returns None on every real blame call
A boundary commit (root commit with no parent) causes `<fixing-commit>~1` to resolve to nothing; plan does not specify the error-handling path for this case
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blame.py file description: 'Invokes `git blame --porcelain <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` so each blamed hunk begins with the 40-char commit SHA on its own line, parsed directly without regex over human-readable output. Returns None if git exits non-zero (including when `<fixing-commit>~1` does not exist, e.g. a root commit).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC #1 (or add a new AC) to assert that the auto-derived `caused_by` link carries `classified` confidence (e.g. `jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .confidence=="classified")'`), so the provenance property is verifiable.
evidence: Approach states: 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative'
AC #1 only checks link presence via `jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'` — does not assert a confidence field value
findings.finding: The Approach section declares 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative,' implying a confidence-level field is set on the written link event, but no AC verifies that the auto-derived edge carries this confidence metadata rather than a higher/default level.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance, never authoritative'; Acceptance Criteria AC #1
scenarios: The implementer writes the link with default/no confidence metadata, all ACs pass, but the stated provenance property is never enforced.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC #1's jq filter to: `jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .confidence=="classified")'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in `blame.py`'s design how path existence at `<fixing-commit>~1` is verified before invoking `git blame` (e.g., `git ls-tree <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` returning empty → treat as rename-uncertain → return None), so the 'failure never blocks close' invariant is mechanically guaranteed.
evidence: file_impact stores paths as of ticket-creation time, which may differ from the path at <fixing-commit>~1 if the fix commit renamed the file
Plan says 'rename/--follow uncertainty' causes None return, but provides no mechanism to detect this condition before or after the git blame invocation
`git blame <sha>~1 -- <path>` fails with exit code 128 if path does not exist at that commit — a capability assertion that is unverified in the plan
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `blame.py` resolves the file path to pass to `git blame <fixing-commit>~1 -- <path>` when the file_impact path may not exist at that commit (e.g., if the fix commit also renamed or deleted the file). The plan mentions '--follow uncertainty' as an ambiguity/bail-out trigger but provides no detection logic for determining whether the path existed at ~1 before attempting the blame call.
findings.location: Files section: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — 'rename/--follow uncertainty'
scenarios: Fix commit renames src/foo.py → src/bar.py and also fixes the bug; file_impact has 'src/foo.py'; git blame HEAD~1 -- src/foo.py succeeds correctly
Fix commit adds a NEW file that contains the bug fix; file_impact has the new file path; git blame HEAD~1 -- <new-path> fails with exit 128 because the file didn't exist at HEAD~1
Fix commit deletes a file and recreates it; path exists at ~1 under the same name but blame returns only the deletion commit's lines

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit proving commands to AC2 and AC3 parallel to AC1, e.g. for AC2: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '[.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")] | length == 0'`; for AC3: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .target=="<id")'`.
evidence: AC1: explicit command `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'`
AC2: 'integration test asserts absence' — no command
AC3: 'test asserts presence' — no command
findings.finding: AC2 ('ambiguous two-commit blame yields no auto caused_by link') and AC3 ('--caused-by <id> sets the link') each say 'integration test asserts absence/presence' but provide no explicit proving command, unlike AC1 which shows a concrete jq invocation. Without a proving command the assertions are unverifiable outside the test file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: AC2 and AC3
scenarios: A reviewer running manual verification after the test suite passes has no command to independently confirm absence/presence of the link
A future executor modifying the test cannot tell what the AC intends to observe without reading the test source
findings.suggested_fix:
AC2 proving command: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '([.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")] | length) == 0'`
AC3 proving command: `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by" and .target=="<override-id>")'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether 'classified'-confidence is a stored LINK event field (and if so, add it to _write_link_event's payload and the dep schema) or a purely rhetorical label (and if so, remove the misleading provenance claim or document it as explanatory-only).
evidence: Plan text: 'Auto-derived edges are classified-confidence provenance, never authoritative.'
_write_link_event writes only {"target_id": target_id, "relation": relation} — no confidence field
common.schema.json dep: {target_id, relation, link_uuid} — no confidence field
CONFIDENCES = frozenset({'high', 'classified'}) is a real closed vocabulary in src/rebar/metrics/registry.py, but it belongs to the metrics system, not the link/dep model
No AC verifies that the auto-derived link is distinguishable from a manually-set one in rebar show output
findings.finding: The plan claims auto-derived 'caused_by' edges carry 'classified'-confidence provenance, but neither the LINK event payload nor the dep schema defines a field to store this confidence label. The existing _write_link_event writes only {target_id, relation}; the dep schema (common.schema.json#/$defs/dep) only has target_id, relation, link_uuid. If 'classified' is a durable, queryable attribute distinguishing auto-derived links from manually-asserted ones, the schema slot and writer call are missing.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-derived edges are classified-confidence provenance, never authoritative.' / src/rebar/graph/_links.py:118-149 (_write_link_event) / src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json#/$defs/dep
scenarios: A consumer queries 'which caused_by links were auto-derived vs. manually set?' — there is no field in the stored event or reduced dep to answer this.
If 'classified' is only a rhetorical label (not stored), the plan misleads implementors who might try to add or query a confidence field that does not exist in the schema.
findings.suggested_fix: If durable provenance tracking is desired, add an optional `confidence` field (enum: ['high', 'classified']) to the dep schema and pass it through _write_link_event; if it is only explanatory, replace 'classified-confidence provenance' with 'best-effort, non-authoritative' in the plan prose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or an `AGENTS.md` / design-doc entry capturing WHY `_write_link_event` is called directly (bypass of closed-source guard is intentional for post-close attribution), so the private-API coupling survives future refactors without silent regression.
evidence: Plan states: 'The auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` **directly**, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
Plan states: 'Same direct-writer path is used for the explicit `--caused-by` override'
No ADR, AGENTS.md change, or design-doc update is listed in the Files section
T5e(a): cross-component private-API coupling is acknowledged but not mitigated via interface/event boundary; T5e(c): novel architectural decision not captured in a durable doc
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate bypass of the closed-source guard by calling the private method `graph._links._write_link_event` directly from `transition_close.py`, but no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is planned to record this architectural decision. Future maintainers who see the private-method call without context may 'fix' it to use the public API (re-introducing the guard blockage), or tighten `_write_link_event` to also enforce the closed-source rule, silently breaking auto-derivation at close time.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`; Approach section paragraph on direct-writer path
scenarios: A maintainer refactoring `_links.py` adds closed-source enforcement inside `_write_link_event` itself, silently breaking auto-link on close
A reviewer sees the private-method call and replaces it with the public `add_dependency` guard path, blocking the close-time link
The bypass rationale is lost when the author leaves the project
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a `## Design notes` section in AGENTS.md) titled something like 'Direct _write_link_event for post-close caused_by links' that records: (1) the closed-source guard exists in `add_dependency`; (2) post-close attribution is intentionally non-blocking; (3) `_write_link_event` is the sanctioned bypass path and must remain guard-free for this use-case. Reference this ADR from a comment in both `transition_close.py` and `_links.py` near the method definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a timeout (e.g., `subprocess.run(..., timeout=10)`) for the `git blame` call in `blame.py` and document that blame is skipped (not retried) on timeout or non-zero exit, so close is never blocked by git unavailability.
evidence: Plan says 'Failure to resolve never blocks the close' but only addresses logic failures (no trailer, ambiguity), not subprocess hangs or git unavailability
No mention of timeout, subprocess deadline, or what happens when `git blame` returns non-zero or hangs
findings.finding: No timeout or circuit-breaker is specified for the `git blame` subprocess call; a hung or slow git process will block the bug-close operation indefinitely, and the plan provides no fallback/degraded mode for this external dependency.
findings.location: src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — Approach section
scenarios: Large monorepo with slow git blame on a hot file blocks every bug-close
git daemon unavailable in CI environment causes all closes to hang
findings.suggested_fix: In `blame.py`, wrap the subprocess call in a try/except with `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` and `subprocess.CalledProcessError`; return `None` on any exception, and emit a debug-level log line naming the failure reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the auto-link write (and the `--caused-by` override write) execute BEFORE the final `push_after_commit(tracker)` call in `close_ticket`, so the LINK commit is included in the same push burst as the STATUS and SNAPSHOT commits.
evidence: close_ticket sequence: transition_core → sign → force-close comment → compact → scratch cleanup → push_after_commit → return
Plan says auto-link runs 'after txn.transition_core has committed the STATUS event' but does not fix its position relative to compact or push_after_commit
push_after_commit is a best-effort call that covers STATUS + compact SNAPSHOT commits
findings.finding: The plan does not specify where in `close_ticket`'s post-commit sequence the auto-link write is inserted. The existing sequence ends with `push_after_commit(tracker)` before returning. If the LINK write happens after `push_after_commit`, the LINK commit will not be included in the same push burst and will remain unpushed until the next write operation.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`; transition_close.py lines 513-523
scenarios: Auto-link fires after push_after_commit: LINK commit is stranded in local branch, not synced to remote until next push-triggering operation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `_lib_writes.py` and `import_ndjson.py` as unenumerated callers of `transition_compute` and confirm `caused_by` will be added as a keyword-only parameter with a default (e.g. `caused_by: str = ""`) so existing call sites remain compatible without modification.
evidence: Plan states 'transition.py — parse --caused-by <id>' implying a new parameter flows through transition_compute
src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:380 calls transition_compute directly
src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:338 and :356 call transition_compute directly
Neither _lib_writes.py nor import_ndjson.py appears in the plan's Files list
findings.finding: transition_compute gains a new `caused_by` parameter, but two callers OUTSIDE _commands/ — `rebar._lib_writes.transition_compute` (src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:380) and `rebar._io.import_ndjson.transition_compute` (src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py:338,356) — are not enumerated as consumers that require a compatibility check (keyword-with-default vs positional change).
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/_commands/transition.py — transition_compute signature change
scenarios: If caused_by is added as a positional arg to transition_compute, _lib_writes.py and import_ndjson.py callers break at runtime with a TypeError.

If caused_by is keyword-only with a default (the likely approach), these callers are silently compatible — but the plan should state this explicitly so the implementer doesn't accidentally add it as positional.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Files section: 'src/rebar/_lib_writes.py — no edit required: caused_by is keyword-only with default "" in transition_compute, so existing call is unaffected.' Same note for import_ndjson.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured debug/info log line in `transition_close.py` (and/or `blame.py`) for each resolution outcome: linked (ticket id), skipped (reason: ambiguous/no-trailer/no-file-impact), and error (exception type), so blame resolution is observable.
evidence: Plan says 'Auto-derived edges are `classified`-confidence provenance' but specifies no logging of resolution outcomes
No metric, log, or trace mentioned anywhere in the plan for blame resolution
findings.finding: No observability is specified for blame resolution outcomes — neither success (link auto-derived), skip (ambiguous/no trailer), nor failure (subprocess error) emits a log, metric, or trace, leaving operators blind to why `caused_by` links are or are not appearing.
findings.location: Approach section / src/rebar/metrics/blame.py / src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py
scenarios: Blame consistently returns None due to a missing `rebar-ticket:` trailer convention; operators see no `caused_by` links and have no log to explain why
A subprocess error silently swallows every blame attempt

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_write_link_event` (or the caller in `transition_close.py`) checks for an existing `caused_by` link between the same source/target before writing, making the write idempotent under retry.
evidence: 'the auto-link therefore writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` **directly**' — no idempotency guard mentioned
Plan notes the STATUS event is already committed before the link write, creating a window where retry could re-run only the link write
findings.finding: The `_write_link_event` direct write is not specified as idempotent: if `transition close` is retried (e.g., after a partial failure after the STATUS event but before the link write completes), a second invocation could write a duplicate `caused_by` link event to the graph store.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — Files section, AC line 1
scenarios: Network blip causes `transition close` to be re-invoked after the STATUS event commits but before the link write; two `caused_by` link events are appended for the same pair
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling `_write_link_event`, query existing links for the bug and skip the write if a `caused_by` link to the same culprit already exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concurrency control mechanism (e.g., file lock around the blame-resolve + write sequence, or a uniqueness check in `_write_link_event`) to prevent duplicate link events when `transition close` is invoked concurrently for the same bug.
evidence: Plan bypasses all existing guards ('directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards') with no compensating concurrency control
No file lock, transaction, or CAS mentioned for the link-write step
findings.finding: No concurrency guard is specified: two simultaneous invocations of `rebar transition <bug> ... closed` (e.g., in a CI fan-out) could both run blame, both resolve the same culprit, and both call `_write_link_event`, producing duplicate link events with no atomic check-and-write.
findings.location: src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py — Approach / Files sections
scenarios: CI pipeline fans out two close invocations for the same bug; both pass the closed-source check (status already committed), both write a caused_by link

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-path test for `--caused-by <invalid-or-nonexistent-id>` (e.g., ticket ID that does not exist in the store) to specify and verify the expected behavior (error message, non-blocking close, or rejection).
evidence: Tests section lists only: '`--caused-by <id>` always sets the link' (valid-ID happy path only)
No AC or test scenario mentions invalid, nonexistent, or malformed ticket IDs passed to `--caused-by`
Rubric: 'a NEW user-facing flow has happy-path-only tests with no failure/timeout/invalid/empty path' = MAJOR
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` parameter (a new user-facing flow) has happy-path-only test coverage: the plan tests that a valid ID sets the link, but no test covers the failure path where `<id>` is invalid, nonexistent, or malformed, leaving the error-handling contract of this new parameter unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Tests section / AC line 3 / src/rebar/_commands/transition.py
scenarios: User typos a ticket ID (`--caused-by TKT-999` where TKT-999 doesn't exist) — undefined behavior, close may silently swallow or crash
CI pipeline passes while live invalid-ID handling is broken
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: '`rebar transition <bug> ... --caused-by NONEXISTENT` either emits a warning and closes without a link, or exits with a clear error — integration test asserts the chosen behavior and that the close itself is not silently broken.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an `_is_active_link(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)` guard before the `_write_link_event` call in the auto-link path (this check is read-only and does not trip the closed-source guard, which is only in `add_dependency`).
evidence: _write_link_event docstring: 'no cycle check, no idempotency'
add_dependency checks _is_active_link before writing (lines 266-270 of _links.py) — this guard is skipped by the plan
close_ticket can be retried (e.g., optimistic-concurrency conflict on the STATUS write) or called twice by test harnesses
findings.finding: `_write_link_event` is explicitly documented as having no idempotency ('no cycle check, no idempotency'). The plan bypasses `add_dependency` (which provides idempotency via `_is_active_link`) to avoid the closed-source guard, but does not add an independent duplicate-link check. A close retry — e.g., after a partial failure in signing or compact — will re-run the auto-link and write a second LINK event for the same `caused_by` edge.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py`; plan phrase 'writes via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` directly'
scenarios: First close attempt: STATUS commits, LINK writes, compact fails → caller retries close → a second LINK event for same (bug, culprit, caused_by) is written
Test suite calls close twice on same fixture: duplicate LINK events accumulate
findings.suggested_fix: In the auto-link block: `if not _is_active_link(bug_id, culprit_id, 'caused_by', tracker_dir): _write_link_event(bug_id, culprit_id, 'caused_by', tracker_dir)`. The same guard should apply to the `--caused-by` explicit override path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Inline `blame.py`'s single-culprit resolver into `transition_close.py` (or move it to `_engine_support/` / `_commands/`) rather than creating a standalone `metrics/blame.py` module with only one call-site; the `metrics/` package is a declarative metric registry and is not the right home for a git-blame dependency-link helper.
evidence: `metrics/__init__.py` docstring: 'This package establishes a standalone, declarative registry of the metrics a future `rebar metrics` command reads once.'
Plan names exactly one consumer of `blame.py`: `src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py` — no other call-site listed or implied.
`src/rebar/metrics/` currently contains only `registry.py` (metric specs) and `snapshot.py` (metric NDJSON store); a blame resolver has no conceptual relation to metrics.
Rule of Three: a new module-level abstraction requires ≥3 call-sites to justify the extraction; the plan names only 1.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` is proposed as a new standalone module with exactly one planned call-site (`transition_close.py`), violating the Rule of Three for a new module-level abstraction. Additionally, `src/rebar/metrics/` is documented as 'a standalone, declarative registry of the metrics a future `rebar metrics` command reads' (see `metrics/__init__.py` docstring), making a git-blame dependency-resolver a conceptual misfit that forces an unrelated utility into the metrics namespace.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`
scenarios: A future maintainer looking for git-blame logic searches `metrics/` and finds it alongside cadence and module-size metrics — a confusing, unmotivated location.
If the blame resolver is never reused, the module boundary adds indirection with no payoff.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the culprit-resolution logic into a private function (e.g., `_resolve_culprit_commit`) inside `transition_close.py`, or place it in `src/rebar/_engine_support/blame.py` if a second call-site is anticipated. Promote to `metrics/blame.py` only when ≥3 call-sites exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` is created with the described pre-fix blame algorithm (`<fixing-commit>~1`, single-dominant-culprit, None on ambiguity/rename/no-trailer), and that the spec explicitly documents the edge-case behavior for root commits and merge commits.
evidence: Plan: 'src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — new: single-culprit resolver. Blames the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fixing-commit>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact'
Directory listing of src/rebar/metrics/ shows only: __init__.py, registry.py, snapshot.py — blame.py is absent
All blame algorithm assertions (dominant culprit, rename/--follow uncertainty, no resolvable trailer returns None) rest on this absent file
findings.finding: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` does not exist in the codebase. The plan names it as the central new file ('new: single-culprit resolver') and pins several behavioral assertions to it (pre-fix parent blame via `<fixing-commit>~1`, dominant culprit algorithm, `rebar-ticket:` trailer mapping, ambiguity/None-return semantics). With the file absent, every codebase behavior claim anchored there is unverifiable.
findings.location: Files section: `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`
scenarios: The blame resolver's conservative ambiguity logic (multi-commit spread → no link) is described but entirely unimplemented and unverifiable; an implementer could produce a more or less aggressive resolver with no plan-grounded constraint.

The `<fixing-commit>~1` pre-fix blaming approach is a non-trivial git operation; without the file, the correctness of this mechanism against edge cases (root commit, merge commits) cannot be verified.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
